facts_disclosed: Lives in a small apartment in the Plateau-Mont-Royal

turns.content: ok I need help because I am about to lose my mind. I want to make a proper beef bourguignon at home tomorrow — like, the real deal, not some shortcut version — and my kitchen is basically working against me

I'm here for it. Beef bourguignon is absolutely doable at home, even in a challenging kitchen. What specifically is working against you? Are we talking limited counter space, equipment issues, oven problems?

all of it honestly. I live in this tiny apartment in the Plateau-Mont-Royal and the kitchen is a joke. Like genuinely smaller than my station at the restaurant. I have maybe two feet of counter space. One burner that actually heats evenly. No ventilation so if I sear anything the smoke alarm goes off and my neighbours hate me

That's a real constraint but it's not a dealbreaker. Plenty of great French cooking has come out of tiny Parisian kitchens that make Montreal apartments look spacious. Let's work with what you have.

First, the searing problem. You can solve the smoke alarm issue by searing the beef in smaller batches at a slightly lower heat -- it'll take longer but you'll still get a good crust without filling the apartment with smoke. Pat the beef very dry beforehand. That reduces the splatter and smoke significantly.

For the counter space: prep everything into bowls first. Mise en place is your best friend here. Once your mirepoix is cut and your beef is portioned, the actual cooking happens in one pot. Do you have a decent Dutch oven?

I have a Le Creuset that barely fits on my stove, oui. It's the one good thing in that kitchen. Everything else is... putain, it's like cooking in a closet. At work I have stations, I have space, I have six burners. At home I'm tripping over myself trying to dice an onion

The Le Creuset is all you really need for this dish -- bourguignon is a one-pot affair once the searing is done. And honestly, the beauty of a braise is that after the initial work, you just let it go for three hours. Your oven does the heavy lifting.

Here's how I'd sequence it in a tight kitchen:

1. Do all your prep first -- dice the mirepoix, cut the beef, peel the pearl onions, slice the mushrooms. Use a cutting board on top of the stove if you need extra space while you're not cooking yet.
2. Sear the beef in the Le Creuset in small batches. Set beef aside in a bowl.
3. Sauté the aromatics in the same pot.
4. Deglaze with the wine, add everything back in, and into the oven.
5. Clean as you go -- by the time it's in the oven, your kitchen is clear.

What wine are you using? That matters more than counter space.

I have a decent Côtes du Rhône. Nothing crazy expensive but it's what I'd actually drink so it should be fine for cooking. The cutting board on the stove trick — I do that already haha, it's survival mode in there. But you're right about the sequencing. At work I'd have all the mise done by a commis and I'd just execute. At home I AM the commis

Côtes du Rhône is a great choice for bourguignon -- you want something with body and fruit but nothing too tannic. That'll work perfectly.

And yes, at home you're the whole brigade. But think of it this way: the mise en place discipline you have from restaurant work is exactly what makes cooking in a tiny kitchen possible. Most home cooks don't prep first and that's when small kitchens become chaotic. You already know how to work clean and organized -- it's just a smaller stage.

One tip for the pearl onions: blanch them first for about a minute to make peeling easier. In a tiny kitchen, fighting with pearl onion skins is the kind of thing that tips you from frustrated to furious. And if you're doing the classic garnish, sauté the mushrooms separately at the end and add them in the last thirty minutes. They'll hold their texture better.

the mushroom thing — yes, 100%. I hate when people just dump them in at the beginning and they turn into rubber. merde. Also — the pearl onions — blanching is obvious but what about glazing them? à brun or à blanc?

For bourguignon, à brun. You want those pearl onions caramelized -- butter, a pinch of sugar, and a splash of stock. Let them go until they're deeply golden and glossy. It adds another layer of sweetness and colour to the finished dish. À blanc is too delicate for something this robust.

Do them in a small sauté pan on your one good burner while the bourguignon is in the oven. Add them in the last 30-40 minutes along with the mushrooms. That way they hold their shape and don't go mushy.

à brun, d'accord. Perfect. Now the real question — I want to do lardons too. Is that overkill for the smoke situation? because the last time I rendered bacon in my apartment I had to open every window and it was -15 outside

Ha, rendering lardons in a Montreal apartment in January with no ventilation -- that's a bold move. But it's worth it. The lardons add a depth that you can't replicate otherwise.

Here's the trick: render them low and slow. I mean genuinely low heat, lower than you'd do at the restaurant. It takes longer but you get the same crispy result without the smoke. Start them in a cold pan, bring the heat up gradually. The fat renders out before the meat crisps, so there's less splatter and less smoke.

Alternatively: render them in the oven. Spread them on a sheet pan at 190C. It takes about 15-20 minutes, the smoke stays contained in the oven, and they come out perfectly crispy. You can pour the rendered fat into the Le Creuset for searing the beef. Best of both worlds.

oven method. That's genius. Ok I'm doing that. You know what kills me though — at work I do dishes ten times more complex than this without even thinking. But at home in this stupid little kitchen everything becomes a project. I keep telling myself I need to move somewhere with a real kitchen but the neighbourhood is so beautiful and I can walk home after service at midnight so... I stay

That walkability after a midnight service is worth a lot -- especially in the Plateau. Most chefs I know would trade a bigger kitchen for a shorter commute in a heartbeat. You spend all day in a professional kitchen anyway. Home is for the occasional passion project and for sleeping.

And honestly, constraints breed creativity. Some of the best cooking happens when you have to problem-solve. You're already adapting -- cutting board on the stove, oven-rendering the lardons. That's the same instinct that makes you good in a restaurant kitchen.

true. And at least the Plateau has everything I need within walking distance — there's a butcher on Mont-Royal who gets me beautiful chuck for this kind of thing. Way better than grocery store meat. I went there today actually

Having a good butcher nearby makes all the difference for a braise. Chuck is the right call -- it has enough marbling and connective tissue to break down beautifully over three hours. Did you ask the butcher to cut it for you, or are you breaking it down yourself?

I had him cut it into big chunks, maybe 5cm. I don't want it too small or it falls apart into nothing. Bigger pieces hold their structure and look better on the plate. Even at home I can't help plating like it's a restaurant, putain I'm hopeless

5cm is perfect -- big enough to maintain integrity after three hours but small enough to cook through evenly. And there's nothing wrong with plating well at home. You eat with your eyes first, even at your own table.

Alright, so we're in good shape. You've got quality chuck, a Le Creuset, a Côtes du Rhône, and a plan. Let me give you the full sequence one more time.

ok c'est bon I'm actually feeling better about this now. So to recap — all the mise first, lardons in the oven, sear beef in small batches low-ish heat, deglaze with the Côtes du Rhône, pearl onions glazed à brun, mushrooms go in at the end. Three hours in the oven. Windows stay closed. Neighbours don't call the fire department

That's the plan. A few last details:

- Add a tablespoon of tomato paste when you sauté the aromatics. Let it cook for a minute until it darkens -- it adds a richness that rounds out the wine.
- A bouquet garni -- thyme, bay leaf, parsley stems -- tied together so you can fish it out easily.
- Season the beef with salt and pepper before searing, and dust lightly with flour. The flour thickens the sauce naturally as it braises.
- Check it at the two-hour mark. The beef should be tender but not falling apart. You want it to hold its shape on the plate.

And use the leftover Côtes du Rhône to drink while it braises. Three hours is a long wait.
haha the wine will not survive three hours, trust me. Merci, this is solid. Tomorrow I conquer the bourguignon and my tiny kitchen does not defeat me. Bonne nuit
Bonne nuit. The bourguignon is going to be great -- and the apartment will smell incredible for days. Enjoy it.
